National Institute of Teaching  ·  Research & Evidence
Evidence Briefing  ·  April 2026

Who Gets a Good Pass? Mapping Disadvantaged Pupils' GCSE Outcomes in English Secondary Schools

Fewer than three in ten disadvantaged pupils achieve a grade 5+ in English and Maths — and the picture is far worse for White British pupils on free school meals.

Calum Davey  ·  April 2026

Full briefing with technical notes available as a PDF

↓ Download PDF

The Gap That Isn't Closing

Every year, fewer than three in ten disadvantaged pupils in England achieve a "good pass" — grade 5 or above in both GCSE English Language and Mathematics. Among their better-off classmates, it is more than five in ten.

That gap — around 27 percentage points — is wider today than it was before the pandemic. Despite billions of pounds in Pupil Premium funding, despite decades of school improvement effort, and despite a stated government commitment to halve it, the gap has barely moved.

But the aggregate figure conceals something important. The disadvantage gap is not the same for all pupils. Ethnicity is a powerful additional factor — and White British pupils on free school meals are among the lowest-performing group in the country.

The National Picture

Good pass: GCSE grade 5+ in both English Language and Mathematics — the government's "strong pass" threshold. Disadvantaged: Pupils eligible for free school meals at any point in the past six years, or looked after (the Pupil Premium definition).

26% Disadvantaged pupils
achieving a good pass
53% Non-disadvantaged pupils
achieving a good pass
27pp Gap between the two groups
(2023–24)
18% White British FSM pupils
achieving a good pass

The Schools White Paper (February 2026) commits to halving this gap — from ~27pp to ~13–14pp. The gap has widened since 2019, not narrowed.

What 3,000 Schools Tell Us

The chart below shows every non-selective secondary school in England with 2024–25 data — around 3,100 schools. Each cell shows the density of schools. The vertical line marks 60% — used here to identify genuinely outstanding performance for disadvantaged pupils.

Most schools cluster in the 10–40% range, consistent with the 26% national average. There is a downward trend — higher pass rates for disadvantaged pupils tend to go with smaller gaps — but the relationship is modest. The 52 schools to the right of the red line are the focus of the next section.

Where Are the High-Performing Schools?

The map shows those 52 schools. Colour = religious character; size = number of disadvantaged pupils.

London dominates — two-thirds of the 52 schools are in the capital. Outside London, examples are scattered in the North West and Yorkshire, with almost none in the Midlands, South West, or East of England. Most belong to strong academy chains: Star Academies (8 schools), Ark, Harris Federation. Half are faith schools.

The Ethnicity Dimension: White British Pupils

White British pupils on free school meals achieve just 18% — the second lowest of any named ethnic group, and far below the 26% average for all disadvantaged pupils.

The chart below shows the grade 5+ good pass rate for FSM-eligible pupils broken down by ethnic group. Diamond markers show the equivalent rate for non-FSM pupils in each group.

Ethnic groupFSM pass rateNon-FSM pass rateGap
Chinese75%79%3pp
Bangladeshi49%61%12pp
Indian49%72%24pp
Black African42%54%12pp
Pakistani36%50%13pp
Caribbean22%37%15pp
White British18%49%31pp
Gypsy/Roma3%13%9pp

Two things stand out. First, the FSM/non-FSM gap for White British pupils is 31pp — among the largest of any group. Being on free school meals is a particularly powerful predictor of low attainment for White British pupils. Second, many minority ethnic groups whose FSM pupils lag the national average still substantially outperform White British FSM pupils.

At school level: the same pattern

The chart below plots each school's disadvantaged pass rate against the proportion of its pupils who are White British.

The downward trend is clear: schools with more White British pupils have lower pass rates for their disadvantaged pupils. Schools with fewer than 20% White British pupils average 40% for disadvantaged pupils; those with more than 37% average just 26%.

This is not simply a London effect or a funding artefact. It reflects the compounding disadvantage faced by White British FSM pupils, who are the largest single group of FSM pupils in England — around 80,000 pupils in the KS4 cohort.

Implications for the National Institute of Teaching

  1. The gap is structural, not just instructional. High-performing schools combine strong curriculum design, coherent CPD, effective Pupil Premium use, and sustained leadership commitment. NIoT's teacher development offer must be embedded in this broader ecology.
  2. The regional distribution is a challenge. Two-thirds of high-performing schools are in London. The areas with the worst outcomes — post-industrial towns, coastal communities, the East Midlands — are those with the highest concentrations of White British disadvantaged pupils. NIoT's national footprint should prioritise these regions.
  3. The chain effect is real. Star Academies, Ark, and Harris have demonstrated improvement at scale in diverse urban schools. Understanding how their teacher development approaches translate — or don't — to predominantly White British school communities is a live question for NIoT.
  4. Ethnicity must be part of the framing. Treating "disadvantaged pupils" as a single group obscures the fact that White British FSM pupils need specific, targeted attention. School improvement strategies that work in diverse urban schools may not transfer to the communities where White British disadvantaged pupils are concentrated.
  5. The 40% target is achievable — but not everywhere equally. The Schools White Paper's ambition to raise disadvantaged pass rates to ~40% is demonstrated as possible by 52 non-selective schools. But those schools are overwhelmingly in London and disproportionately diverse. Reaching 40% in towns like Barnsley, Stoke, or Grimsby is a different challenge.

Technical Notes

School-level data: DfE KS4 Performance tables, 2024–25 (provisional). "Good pass" = grade 5+ in both English Language and Mathematics GCSE. "Disadvantaged" = ever-FSM in past 6 years + looked-after children. Non-selective schools via GIAS admissions policy. n = 3,110 schools with non-suppressed data.

National figures (26%/53%/27pp): DfE KS4 2023–24 — most recent published national breakdown by disadvantage group.

Ethnicity × FSM data: Social Mobility Commission / DfE Attainment at Age 16 dataset, 2022–23. School-level White British proportions from DfE Schools, Pupils and Their Characteristics 2024–25 (January 2025 census). Joined by URN; 3,232 schools matched.

Map: England LA boundaries from martinjc/UK-GeoJSON. Coordinates from GIAS (OSGB36 → WGS84). Analysis: Python (pandas, matplotlib, pyproj). April 2026.

Prepared April 2026  ·  Data: Department for Education, GIAS, Social Mobility Commission
